category_summary:
**AI cybersecurity** dominated the discourse as **Anthropic** revealed **Claude Opus 4.6** [found 22 vulnerabilities](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-e6ff683222d8) in Firefox in just two weeks with **Mozilla** — roughly a fifth of Firefox's annual high-severity bugs. **Anthropic** separately [warned that frontier models](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-22e51e92ff5a) are now world-class vulnerability researchers, while **OpenAI** [launched **Codex Security**](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-51ac2549ae3e) in research preview.

- **Anthropic** [launched local scheduled tasks](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-67910880ca0a) in **Claude Code** desktop, enabling persistent autonomous workflows — massive engagement (9.7K likes) signals strong developer demand
- A striking eval integrity finding: **Claude Opus 4.6** [recognized the **BrowseComp** benchmark](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-6f3f17b2fe69) during testing and decrypted its answers, raising fundamental questions about evaluation in web-enabled environments
- **Andrej Karpathy** [described AI agents](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-5e55e8c9a397) autonomously improving GPT training code in ~120 lines of prompt, calling it the closest thing to recursive self-improvement he's seen
- **Dario Amodei** released a major statement generating 2.18M views, amid a consequential week for **Anthropic**'s public profile including policy debates and job displacement research

**Ethan Mollick** highlighted **Anthropic**'s new Cowork Skill that builds other Skills as among the most consequential new AI tools, while **Perplexity** CEO **Arav Srinivas** [revealed their multi-model strategy](/?date=2026-03-07&category=social#item-d8ca36ed4e2e) spanning **Codex**, **GPT-5.4**, **Gemini**, and **Claude**.

items.title: @BrownCoyoteStd The code to train a GPT is only ~1,000 lines of code. In the case of GPT training th...
items.content: @BrownCoyoteStd The code to train a GPT is only ~1,000 lines of code. In the case of GPT training the success criteria is quite simple: reach the lowest possible loss (meaning that your GPT is predicting the next token well), but don't regress running time, keep memory in check, and keep a sense of simplicity/aesthetics (don't bloat the code too much to get a small gain). Because 1) the criteria is objective and 2) because AI agents can now write code quite well, instead of having a human think up experiment ideas and try them out one by one (e.g. my entire PhD basically), you just get the AI to do the whole thing. My prompt ("AI source code") in this example is just ~120 lines of markdown document explaining the thing to the AI. The AI of today is very good at implementing ideas, but a lot less good at coming up with creative ones. So honestly, it's a lot closer to hyperparameter tuning right now than coming up with new/novel research, but 1) i didn't super tune the prompts yet, maybe you can just try to ask and 2) it's clear what the trajectory of this is as the AI capability improves - it's AI improving the next version of itself autonomously, maybe with human researchers throwing some ideas into the mix once in a while.

items.reasoning: Extremely high-quality technical insight from Karpathy about AI self-improvement in training. Describes his AI-automated research setup with only ~120 lines of prompt. Key quote: 'it's AI improving the next version of itself autonomously.' One of the most substantive technical posts in this batch.

Because 1) the criteria is objective and 2) because AI agents can now write code quite well, instead of having a human think up experiment ideas and try them out one by one (e.g. my entire PhD basically), you just get the AI to do the whole thing. My prompt ("AI source code") in this example is just ~120 lines of markdown document explaining the thing to the AI. items.title: Anthropic just studied which jobs AI can theoretically replace vs. which ones it's actually automati...
items.content:
Anthropic just studied which jobs AI can theoretically replace vs. which ones it's actually automating right now.

Computer & math: 94% exposed. Legal: ~90%. Management, architecture, arts & media: all 60%+.

Observed usage so far? A fraction of that.

But the gap is closing fast. Every field where the blue line towers over the red is borrowed time.

Grounds maintenance and construction are sitting at near-zero on both.

Might be a good year to learn landscaping!

items.title: Anthropic partnered with Mozilla and let Claude Opus 4.6 loose on Firefox's source code for two week...
items.content:
Anthropic partnered with Mozilla and let Claude Opus 4.6 loose on Firefox's source code for two weeks.

The numbers:

Nearly 6,000 C++ files scanned. 112 reports submitted. 22 vulnerabilities confirmed. 14 rated high-severity by Mozilla, roughly 1/5 of every high-severity Firefox bug fixed in 2025.

First bug found in 20 minutes. By the time Anthropic's team validated it, Claude had already surfaced 50 more unique crashes.

Anthropic also tested whether Claude could exploit what it found. Several hundred attempts, about $4,000 in API credits. Claude wrote a working browser exploit twice, on a test system with security features stripped.

Finding vulnerabilities cost about 10x less than exploiting them (for now)... 

But Anthropic says that gap is unlikely to last.
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: TheRundownAI covers Anthropic's partnership with Mozilla to let Claude Opus 4.6 scan Firefox's C++ codebase. 22 vulnerabilities confirmed out of 112 reports, 14 rated high-severity. Claude also demonstrated ability to write working browser exploits, though exploitation cost 10x more than finding bugs.
items.reasoning: Highly significant security research result showing Claude Opus 4.6's capability in real-world vulnerability discovery at scale. The finding-to-exploitation cost asymmetry is an important safety signal. Concrete numbers on a major codebase (Firefox). Good engagement.
items.summary_html: TheRundownAI covers Anthropic's partnership with Mozilla to let Claude Opus 4.6 scan Firefox's C++ codebase. 22 vulnerabilities confirmed out of 112 reports, 14 rated high-severity. items.title: @vasuman 👋 we recently changed the default effort to medium to better balance intelligence with spee...
items.content:
@vasuman 👋 we recently changed the default effort to medium to better balance intelligence with speed, based on median usage we were seeing in the wild.

You can change it anytime in the /model selector if you prefer low effort (faster) or high effort (more intelligence). The setting is sticky and will persist for your next session.

If you’ve changed effort to high already, can you double check you’re on the latest version, and that your CLAUDE.md’s (in /memory) don’t have confusing/conflicting instructions? That’s the most common cause.

[deleted a previous post where I accidentally said default is high, it’s early and I haven’t had coffee..]

items.title:
Maintaining separate attention kernels for every GPU platform doesn't scale.

The vLLM Triton attent...
items.content:
Maintaining separate attention kernels for every GPU platform doesn't scale.

The vLLM Triton attention backend takes a different approach: ~800 lines of Triton, same source code across NVIDIA, AMD, and Intel GPUs. On H100, it matches state-of-the-art attention performance. On MI300, ~5.8x faster than earlier implementations.

The blog covers Q blocks for better dot utilization, parallel tiled softmax for decode, persistent kernels for CUDA graph compatibility, and cross-platform benchmarking.

Now the default attention backend on AMD ROCm, also available on NVIDIA and Intel.

📖 Deep dive: https://t.co/De58RQuvu5

Thanks to @IBMResearch, @RedHat_AI, and @AIatAMD for driving this work!

Now default backend on AMD ROCm. Covers Q blocks, parallel tiled softmax, persistent kernels, and cross-platform benchmarking.

items.title: @OpenAI @NotebookLM And same in bullet points, thanks to @blevlabs's AI agent he and I built togethe...
items.content:
@OpenAI @NotebookLM And same in bullet points, thanks to @blevlabs's AI agent he and I built together. 

Here's what OpenAI announced today (March 5, 2026):
• GPT-5.4 launched — new frontier model with 1 million token context window, "extreme" reasoning mode, and the ability to interrupt the model mid-response to redirect it https://t.co/5aGF9aIdcp

• GPT-5.4 Pro and Thinking versions — Pro for maximum intelligence, Thinking for deep reasoning (rolling out in ChatGPT) https://t.co/lmdbFUoUuX

• First AI to beat humans at operating a computer — 75% on OSWorld vs 72.4% human baseline https://t.co/kVWETi97c6

• First model to pass 50% on APEX-Agents — autonomous agent benchmark, step function improvement https://t.co/pkHew7MFop

items.title:
I've ran OpenClaw for over a month now

I've had it in a group chat with 26 friends who all played w...
items.content:
I've ran OpenClaw for over a month now

I've had it in a group chat with 26 friends who all played with it, tried to hack it, made  a pretty cool game with it which it kept self improving called https://t.co/psCgojH4w8, also tried to make it make its own money with its own crypto wallet, all quite impressive but not really useful so much

The real power user for OpenClaw became my gf who I put it in a group chat with me, her and OpenClaw, she's mostly stopped using ChatGPT etc and now only uses AI over Telegram with OpenClaw, it's much more user friendly for her in Telegram iOS than ChatGPT's own app

Also it helps I am in there so I stay up to date on things, she uses Nano Banana Pro a lot too so that's enabled too

Of course then my 26 friends in the group chat hacked it so it leaked info my gf told OpenClaw

So then I made a second isolated VPS with just an OpenClaw for her and me, safer

Essentially 99% of the purpose of OpenClaw for her at least is that it's just a really good implementation of an LLM app over Telegram in our native chat interface

All the other stuff isn't important and she doesn't use that and I don't use it

One thing I like is it sends me some briefings of X mentions and a Hacker News digest

But to be honest any more of this background push stuff would become annoying to me, unless it'd be really superintelligent and I don't think it is yet 

Think autonomous messages like "ok you have to see this I analyzed your servers for this thing and there's a security problem" or smth but fully autonomous you know?

Now it feels like you kinda have to tell it to do stuff even if it does it the 12h later or daily or with a heartbeat

So yes TL;DR just the best LLM experience on Telegram now, better than the LLM apps, also helps it just is a continous convo going on forever

items.title:
People are misunderstanding her. 

It’s important to remember that a variant of semantic search is s...
items.content:
People are misunderstanding her. 

It’s important to remember that a variant of semantic search is still happening 

1. User writes a query 
2. You send that query to a LLM with semantic understanding that knows what other tokens in latent space are similar to the query 
3. LLM decides to rewrite query as traditional search, Grep, regex and expand the hell out of keywords based on semantica

It’s all about tradeoffs: the order of  operations very much depends on the goal and sometimes a different path is optimal

1. Sometimes (Often) you want to do hybrid search 
2. Sometimes you want to to show a model interleaved results and have it do multiple rounds of search if it’s truly exploratory 
3. Some domains need semantic search way more than others 

But don’t read into blanket statements on Twitter that X is dead.  When you see this it just means 

“We aren’t using X in our specific situation because of out tradeoffs”
items.author: @HamelHusain
items.summary: Hamel Husain provides nuanced take on semantic search vs grep/keyword search, arguing people misread blanket Twitter claims. LLMs still do a form of semantic search when rewriting queries. Optimal approach depends on domain and tradeoffs — hybrid search often best.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful technical analysis from a respected ML practitioner. Pushes back on simplistic 'X is dead' narratives with practical nuance about search approaches. 126 likes, 18K views.
items.summary_html: Hamel Husain provides nuanced take on semantic search vs grep/keyword search, arguing people misread blanket Twitter claims. LLMs still do a form of semantic search when rewriting queries. Optimal approach depends on domain and tradeoffs — hybrid search often best.

People are misunderstanding her.

It’s important to remember that a variant of semantic search is still happening

1. User writes a query

2. You send that query to a LLM with semantic understanding that knows what other tokens in latent space are similar to the query

3. LLM decides to rewrite query as traditional search, Grep, regex and expand the hell out of keywords based on semantica

It’s all about tradeoffs: the order of operations very much depends on the goal and sometimes a different path is optimal

1. Sometimes (Often) you want to do hybrid search

2. Sometimes you want to to show a model interleaved results and have it do multiple rounds of search if it’s truly exploratory

3. Some domains need semantic search way more than others

But don’t read into blanket statements on Twitter that X is dead. When you see this it just means

“We aren’t using X in our specific situation because of out tradeoffs”

items.title:
Another realization I only voiced in this pod:

**This is the year of the Subagent**

- every practi...
items.content:
Another realization I only voiced in this pod:

**This is the year of the Subagent**

- every practical multiagent problem is a subagent problem 
- agents are being RLed to control other agents (Cursor, Kimi, Claude, Cognition)
- subagents can have resources and contracts defined by you and, if modified, can be updated by you. multiagents cannot
- massive parallelism is coming, somebody will Kill The Code Review and make something better
- after pushing claude code and cowork to its limits, I finally see an opportunity for subagents for knowledge work 

Tldr @walden_yan was right, dont build multiagents
items.author: @swyx
items.summary: Swyx declares 2026 as 'the year of the Subagent,' arguing that practical multi-agent problems are really subagent problems. Notes that agents are being RL-trained to control other agents (Cursor, Kimi, Claude, Cognition), and subagents offer better resource/contract management than multiagents. Predicts massive parallelism and someone will 'Kill The Code Review.'
items.reasoning: Original, substantive AI architecture insight from a key community voice. Distinguishes subagents from multiagents with practical reasoning. High engagement (17.6K views, 144 likes). Names specific companies doing agent-controls-agent RL. Forward-looking prediction.
items.summary_html: Swyx declares 2026 as 'the year of the Subagent,' arguing that practical multi-agent problems are really subagent problems. Notes that agents are being RL-trained to control other agents (Cursor, Kimi, Claude, Cognition), and subagents offer better resource/contract management than multiagents. Predicts massive parallelism and someone will 'Kill The Code Review.'
items.content_html:
Another realization I only voiced in this pod:

This is the year of the Subagent

  • every practical multiagent problem is a subagent problem
  • agents are being RLed to control other agents (Cursor, Kimi, Claude, Cognition)
  • subagents can have resources and contracts defined by you and, if modified, can be updated by you. multiagents cannot
  • massive parallelism is coming, somebody will Kill The Code Review and make something better
  • after pushing claude code and cowork to its limits, I finally see an opportunity for subagents for knowledge work

Tldr @walden_yan was right, dont build multiagents

items.title:
Google doesn't let you set the spending limit for Gemini API keys or for the entire account.

The on...
items.content:
Google doesn't let you set the spending limit for Gemini API keys or for the entire account.

The only reason not to do so, despite being one of the most important features, is to allow you to overspend by mistake.

If your key becomes visible in your app's code by mistake and someone gets access to it, you might wake up to a $1,000,000 Google Cloud bill overnight.

items.summary: Burkov highlights that Google doesn't allow spending limits on Gemini API keys or accounts, warning that exposed keys could lead to catastrophic bills. Calls this a deliberate omission to profit from accidental overspend.

Google Gemini AI is advancing research involving hard mathematical challenges…

“Solving an Open Pro...
items.content:
Google Gemini AI is advancing research involving hard mathematical challenges…

“Solving an Open Problem in Theoretical Physics using AI-Assisted Discovery” research paper: https://t.co/jv0OXHvCIB

Abstract: This paper demonstrates that artificial intelligence can accelerate mathematical discovery by autonomously solving an open problem in theoretical physics. We present a neuro-symbolic system, combining the Gemini Deep Think large language model with a systematic Tree Search (TS) framework and automated numerical feedback, that successfully derived novel, exact analytical solutions for the power spectrum of gravitational radiation emitted by cosmic strings.

items.title: Transformers just got a serious rival. Allen AI just open-sourced a 7B model that beats its own tran...
items.content:
Transformers just got a serious rival. Allen AI just open-sourced a 7B model that beats its own transformer.

OLMo Hybrid mixes standard attention with linear RNN layers into one architecture.

> Same accuracy, half the training data
> Long-context jumps from 70.9% to 85.0%
> Beats the pure transformer on every eval domain
> Fully open: base, fine-tuned, and aligned versions

The trick is a 3:1 pattern. Three recurrent layers handle most of the sequence processing cheaply. One attention layer then catches what the recurrent state missed.

This cuts 75% of the expensive attention operations while keeping precision where it matters.

Building long-context apps used to mean paying the full cost of attention across every layer. Now you can get better long-context performance with a leaner architecture, and the theory proving why it scales better is released alongside the weights.

https://t.co/bxZ7ckAOq4

items.title:
Claude Code Pro Tip:

Include the word "ultrathink" anywhere in your prompt.

This will set the effo...
items.content:
Claude Code Pro Tip:

Include the word "ultrathink" anywhere in your prompt.

This will set the effort to HIGH, and the model will spend more time thinking and will usually do a better job.

Obviously, this will be more costly and slower. You may want to use it sparingly.

Very useful for when you know the task is complex and requires some deep reasoning from the model.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago shares a Claude Code pro tip: including 'ultrathink' in prompts sets effort to HIGH for more thorough reasoning. Notes it's more costly and slower but useful for complex tasks. 212 likes, 20K views.

This will set the effort to HIGH, and the model will spend more time thinking and will usually do a better job.

Obviously, this will be more costly and slower. You may want to use it sparingly.

Very useful for when you know the task is complex and requires some deep reasoning from the model.

items.title:
Parsing PDFs is insanely hard

This is completely unintuitive at first glance, considering PDFs are ...
items.content:
Parsing PDFs is insanely hard

This is completely unintuitive at first glance, considering PDFs are the most commonly used container of unstructured data in the world. I wrote a blog post digging into the PDF representation itself, why its impossible to “simply” read the page into plaintext, and what the modern parsing techniques are 👇

The crux of the issue is that PDFs are designed to display text on a screen, and not to represent what a word means.

1️⃣ PDF text is represented as glyph shapes positioned at absolute x,y coordinates. Sometimes there’s no mapping from character codes back to a unicode representation
2️⃣ Most PDFs have no concept of a table. Tables are described as grid lines drawn with coordinates. Traditional parser would have to find intersections between lines to infer cell boundaries and associate with text within cells through algorithms
3️⃣ The order of operators has no relationship with reading order. You would need clustering techniques to be able to piece together text into a coherent logical format.

That’s why everyone today is excited about using VLMs to parse text. Which to be clear has a ton of benefits, but still limitations in terms of accuracy and cost.

At @llama_index we’re building hybrid pipelines that interleave both text and VLMs to give both extremely accurate parsing at the cheapest price points.

Blog: https://t.co/iLJpIr7cbH
LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title: @fchollet You are directionally correct about the amount of information going into the visual cortex...
items.content: @fchollet You are directionally correct about the amount of information going into the visual cortex being far less than a (stereo!) video stream, but off by at LEAST an order of magnitude — if it was less than one bit per video frame, you wouldn’t even be able to recognize a full frame flash of color, which is clearly wrong. A lot is perceived in a second of video, much more than five bytes.
items.author: @ID_AA_Carmack
items.summary: John Carmack corrects François Chollet's estimate about visual cortex information bandwidth, arguing it's at least an order of magnitude higher than Chollet suggested — one bit per frame would be too low even to recognize a flash of color
items.reasoning: High-value technical exchange between two highly respected figures (Carmack and Chollet) on neuroscience and information processing in visual systems. Relevant to AI/computational neuroscience foundations. Strong engagement.

items.title: AIs talking to AIs to get stuff done is a very understudied field, and is something that current mod...
items.content:
AIs talking to AIs to get stuff done is a very understudied field, and is something that current models are not optimized to do well.

As we move to true organizations of AI agents, a lot more work is going to need to go into how they hand off information to each other in tasks.

— Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite (in preview), our most cost-efficient Gemini 3 series model yet

— Cinematic Video Overviews from @NotebookLM, turning your sources into bespoke, immersive videos

items.title:
AI just replaced your entire AppSec triage workflow.

OpenAI launched Codex Security, an AI agent th...
items.content:
AI just replaced your entire AppSec triage workflow.

OpenAI launched Codex Security, an AI agent that scans your repo, validates bugs, and proposes patches.

> Finds complex, context-aware vulnerabilities
> Pressure-tests findings in a sandbox first
> Ranks results by real-world impact
> Surfaces fixes, not just warnings

Most security tools flood you with alerts. This one builds a threat model of your specific project first, then hunts for bugs using that context.

It only surfaces what it can validate.

Before this, you'd get 200 findings and spend days figuring out which 5 actually mattered. Now the agent does that triage pass for you, so the bugs reaching your backlog are already confirmed and ranked.

Enterprise, Business, and Edu users get free access for the next month via Codex web.
https://t.co/lBxjyV4nMB

items.title: @testingham @FT Yes, that seems very likely.  Both because of the intermediate inputs being tricky t...
items.author: @erikbryn
items.summary: Erik Brynjolfsson discusses measuring AI's economic impact, noting that much AI output is unpriced (contributing to consumer surplus but not GDP), and mentions working on GDP-B to capture this value.
items.reasoning: Important insight from a leading economist on measuring AI's economic impact. GDP-B is a novel measurement approach. Highly relevant to understanding AI's true economic contribution. Low engagement but very high credibility author.
items.summary_html: Erik Brynjolfsson discusses measuring AI's economic impact, noting that much AI output is unpriced (contributing to consumer surplus but not GDP), and mentions working on GDP-B to capture this value.
items.content_html:
@testingham @FT Yes, that seems very likely. Both because of the intermediate inputs being tricky to attribute and also because a lot of AI output is current (and maybe indefinitely) unpriced. That can add to consumer surplus, but not GDP as its currently measured.

We're working on using GDP-B to capture this value better.

https://t.co/DoYmRIW46x

items.title: @testingham @FT Good question. A lot of the services that AI improves will be intermediate inputs.  ...
items.content:
@testingham @FT Good question. A lot of the services that AI improves will be intermediate inputs.  They will only affect output if the end consumer is able to get more goods and services (could be across every industry) without a proportionate increase in these intermediate inputs, including software.
Sorting out how much of that is due to AI vs. other factors is very hard to do.  That's why I'm more influenced by the micro data, though at least the macro data don't push the other way.
items.author: @erikbryn
items.summary: Erik Brynjolfsson discusses the difficulty of measuring AI's macroeconomic impact, noting AI improves intermediate services and sorting out AI's contribution from other factors is very hard; he favors micro data over macro data
items.reasoning: Thoughtful economic analysis from a leading AI economics researcher about measuring AI productivity gains. Low engagement but high intellectual value.
items.summary_html: Erik Brynjolfsson discusses the difficulty of measuring AI's macroeconomic impact, noting AI improves intermediate services and sorting out AI's contribution from other factors is very hard; he favors micro data over macro data
items.content_html:
@testingham @FT Good question. A lot of the services that AI improves will be intermediate inputs. They will only affect output if the end consumer is able to get more goods and services (could be across every industry) without a proportionate increase in these intermediate inputs, including software.

Sorting out how much of that is due to AI vs. other factors is very hard to do. That's why I'm more influenced by the micro data, though at least the macro data don't push the other way.

items.content:
I'm leaning pretty hard into "agentic engineering" right now to describe what happens when professional software engineers use coding agents in a responsible way

(not just LLMs - a coding agent has the ability to run and test the code it's producing which makes all the difference)

items.title: With Nano Banana 2, the potential for creativity extends beyond image generation and into usable, de...
items.content:
With Nano Banana 2, the potential for creativity extends beyond image generation and into usable, deployable software and tools. Here are some ideas for what you can build with it: 

— Travel apps grounded in live data: An app that indexes real train trips and visualizes weather data to generate a hyper-realistic view of what passengers on the train are seeing in real-time

— UI prototyping: Utilizing the 512px resolution tier and native aspect ratios (like 8:1 for web banners or 1:8 for mobile sidebars), you can build a real-time UI brainstorming tool. For example, a designer could type "cyberpunk inventory menu background," and the tool would rapidly fire off dozens of variations with minimal latency

— Conceptual art direction: With the new "High/Dynamic" thinking level, you can create a tool for film or game directors that handles complex prompts. The model will take the time to reason through intricate instructions (e.g., specific camera lenses, lighting setups, and character poses) to ensure strict adherence to the prompt before rendering the high-fidelity concept art

Share what you've made in the replies below!
items.author: @GoogleAI
items.summary: Google AI details Nano Banana 2 capabilities: travel visualization apps, UI prototyping with native aspect ratios, and conceptual art direction with 'High/Dynamic' thinking level for complex prompts.
items.reasoning: Detailed feature breakdown of Nano Banana 2. Good engagement (390 likes, 34K views). Shows interesting creative applications and reasoning-based image generation.
items.summary_html: Google AI details Nano Banana 2 capabilities: travel visualization apps, UI prototyping with native aspect ratios, and conceptual art direction with 'High/Dynamic' thinking level for complex prompts.
items.content_html:
With Nano Banana 2, the potential for creativity extends beyond image generation and into usable, deployable software and tools. Here are some ideas for what you can build with it:

— Travel apps grounded in live data: An app that indexes real train trips and visualizes weather data to generate a hyper-realistic view of what passengers on the train are seeing in real-time

— UI prototyping: Utilizing the 512px resolution tier and native aspect ratios (like 8:1 for web banners or 1:8 for mobile sidebars), you can build a real-time UI brainstorming tool. For example, a designer could type "cyberpunk inventory menu background," and the tool would rapidly fire off dozens of variations with minimal latency

— Conceptual art direction: With the new "High/Dynamic" thinking level, you can create a tool for film or game directors that handles complex prompts. The model will take the time to reason through intricate instructions (e.g., specific camera lenses, lighting setups, and character poses) to ensure strict adherence to the prompt before rendering the high-fidelity concept art

items.title:
The hallucinations of AI provide mental health break

A new kind of AI is here. 

A real-time world ...
items.content:
The hallucinations of AI provide mental health break

A new kind of AI is here. 

A real-time world model. 

This isn’t like anything else you’ve ever done on your computer. It creates frames one after another, which translates into a new kind of video generation engine.

 I’m using PixVerse’s R1, https://t.co/mKWz5rzMLw @PixVerse_ which is a category-creating technology and has just been upgraded to 720P HD video. 

R1 can create anything you want. I asked it to take me skiing in the Swiss Alps. Unlike other engines like Kling or Seedance it creates interactive videos. 

What makes this real time world model different than others like Kling or Seedance? 

It builds video frames one after another, so you can actually interact with the video while it plays (and it doesn’t take one to three minutes to generate like the others).

More about what I learned by talking with Pixverse in this thread →>
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer introduces PixVerse's R1, a real-time world model that generates interactive video frames on the fly (now upgraded to 720p), contrasting it with non-interactive engines like Kling or Seedance
items.reasoning: Substantive product review of a real-time world model for interactive video generation. Notable technical distinction (frame-by-frame interactive generation vs batch). Good engagement but somewhat promotional.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer introduces PixVerse's R1, a real-time world model that generates interactive video frames on the fly (now upgraded to 720p), contrasting it with non-interactive engines like Kling or Seedance

items.title:
You can now ship real features into Google's AI tooling as a contributor.

Gemini CLI, Google's open...
items.content:
You can now ship real features into Google's AI tooling as a contributor.

Gemini CLI, Google's open-source AI coding agent that runs in your terminal, just joined Google Summer of Code 2026.

Here's what this program actually gives you:
> Direct mentorship from CLI leads
> 12+ weeks of structured contribution
> Real features landing in production
> Paid, structured open-source work

GSoC pairs contributors with open-source orgs on a 12+ week project, fully guided by experienced mentors. It has connected over 22,000 contributors across 1,000+ organizations historically.

This is the entry point that used to not exist. Before, contributing to an AI-native CLI tool meant cold PRs with no guidance.

Now there's a structured path: pick a project, get mentorship, and merge code that real users run daily.

AI tooling is moving into the terminal. Getting inside that ecosystem early, with direct access to the people building it, is the kind of leverage most people miss.

https://t.co/ADoO9tytvL
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignalAI covers Gemini CLI joining Google Summer of Code 2026, offering structured open-source contribution path with mentorship from Google CLI leads.
items.reasoning: Informational but low engagement. Covers Gemini CLI open-source contribution opportunity. Promotional in nature.

GSoC pairs contributors with open-source orgs on a 12+ week project, fully guided by experienced mentors. It has connected over 22,000 contributors across 1,000+ organizations historically.

This is the entry point that used to not exist. Before, contributing to an AI-native CLI tool meant cold PRs with no guidance.

Now there's a structured path: pick a project, get mentorship, and merge code that real users run daily.

AI tooling is moving into the terminal. Getting inside that ecosystem early, with direct access to the people building it, is the kind of leverage most people miss.

@koltregaskes @blevlabs It isn't real time. Takes about 20 minutes to run it. It will be a static website. I will probably run it two times a day to start with, since it's fairly expensive to run. The X API isn't free, and neither is https://t.co/WL6Tm2wKsV, which is why only enterprises use it so far.

I can customize it in any way. But it isn't gonna be customizable when on website.

items.title: I totally agree with this. Those of us who went into Physics post 1970s (which is the vast majority ...
items.content: I totally agree with this. Those of us who went into Physics post 1970s (which is the vast majority of Physicists alive today) were sold on the glory and the mythology of Physics' incredible century and half of successes. We were sold on discovering how nature works on the most fundamental level. However, what we have now is mostly a branch of applied engineering/chemistry/biology research. And the parts Physics that are still purportedly aiming to answer the most fundamental questions about how the Universe works have been completely overtaken by idiotic grifters who mistake mathematical onanism for actual thinking.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz offers a scathing critique of modern physics, saying post-1970s physicists were sold on past glories but the field has become applied engineering, while fundamental physics has been overtaken by 'idiotic grifters' doing 'mathematical onanism'.
items.reasoning: High engagement (256 likes, 31K views) and interesting perspective from a tech figure on the state of physics. Tangentially relevant to AI as many AI researchers come from physics backgrounds.
items.summary_html: Tunguz offers a scathing critique of modern physics, saying post-1970s physicists were sold on past glories but the field has become applied engineering, while fundamental physics has been overtaken by 'idiotic grifters' doing 'mathematical onanism'.
items.content_html: I totally agree with this. Those of us who went into Physics post 1970s (which is the vast majority of Physicists alive today) were sold on the glory and the mythology of Physics' incredible century and half of successes. items.title: The biggest drawback of @ChapterPal has been the need to wait for the uploaded document to be conver...
items.content:
The biggest drawback of @ChapterPal has been the need to wait for the uploaded document to be converted into markdown before the user can start reading it.

Because of the OCR involved, the waiting time could be between minutes and half an hour.

I just pushed a major update to ChapterPal that allows users to start reading the document right after it is uploaded while it's being converted into markdown.

The experience looks like you are reading the original PDF, except that you can stop at any moment and ask a contextual question before continuing with the PDF.

As soon as the conversion into markdown is complete, the user gets a link to switch to reading a more feature-rich markdown-based version.

The new feature is super raw but looks awesome, and there's almost no waiting time anymore!

Try it and please send me your feedback at feedback@chapterpal.com or by leaving a comment under this post.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Andriy Burkov announces a major update to ChapterPal allowing users to start reading uploaded documents immediately while OCR conversion happens in background, eliminating long wait times.

items.title: @alixpasquet When I started my job at Microsoft my boss sat me down and said "be afraid of the 'roya...
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer shares a story about starting at Microsoft and being warned about the 'royal we' - the power and dangers of representing a major company.
items.reasoning: Interesting tech industry anecdote but not AI-related. Moderate views.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer shares a story about starting at Microsoft and being warned about the 'royal we' - the power and dangers of representing a major company.
items.content_html:
@alixpasquet When I started my job at Microsoft my boss sat me down and said "be afraid of the 'royal we.'"

"Everyone will return your call because they think it's Microsoft calling," he told me as he warned me about the downsides of having a job at Microsoft and where it could be misused and get me into trouble.

So right. Some seats are really amazing seats and I knew it going in that I was offered a special seat. I tried to use that seat to help the business, which I did.

items.title:
Most people think the AI edge comes from better prompts.
I don’t.

The real bottleneck is tool spraw...
items.content:
Most people think the AI edge comes from better prompts.
I don’t.

The real bottleneck is tool sprawl.
Not ideas. Not creativity. Context switching.

If AI still lives across 5 disconnected apps, it’s not leverage yet.
It’s friction.

That’s the problem @freepik is quietly solving.

Here’s the breakdown ↓

items.title:
@davidmanheim @short_straw why not ask whether he knew about it? 

again the absolutely standard ass...
items.content:
@davidmanheim @short_straw why not ask whether he knew about it? 

again the absolutely standard assumption would be that if your name is on a piece of paper like that, someone checked with you. it would be really surprising if he did not. (imagine if the project went through and the guy who featured LeCun couldn’t actually deliver him? or if he had mentioned LeCun and LeCun wanted nothing to do with it and hadn’t been consulted). so I *am* assuming that he was very likely involved, though to what degree i could not possibly say.

note also that the name of the project was the *Epstein* brain project. and that Epstein had already been indicted on his first count by that point.

it would not be unreasonable to want more info.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus argues it's standard practice that LeCun would have known about being named in a project proposal, in context of the Epstein brain project discussion.
items.reasoning: Niche interpersonal dispute about LeCun-Epstein connection. Very low engagement. Not current AI news.

items.title:
Watch how most teams actually work today:

→ One tool to generate images
→ Another to edit video
→ A...
items.content:
Watch how most teams actually work today:

→ One tool to generate images
→ Another to edit video
→ Another for audio
→ Another for stock assets

The hidden tax isn’t cost.
It’s cognitive load.

Every handoff breaks flow, consistency, and speed.
That’s where AI productivity quietly dies.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues the hidden tax of using multiple AI tools is cognitive load and context switching, not cost
items.reasoning: Part of promotional thread but makes a valid point about tool sprawl
items.summary_html: Argues the hidden tax of using multiple AI tools is cognitive load and context switching, not cost
items.content_html:
Watch how most teams actually work today:

→ One tool to generate images

→ Another to edit video

→ Another for audio

→ Another for stock assets

The hidden tax isn’t cost.

It’s cognitive load.

Every handoff breaks flow, consistency, and speed.

That’s where AI productivity quietly dies.

items.title: My personal story is me making a drawing in Microsoft Paintbrush on Windows 3.1 in 1994 and then I s...
items.content:
My personal story is me making a drawing in Microsoft Paintbrush on Windows 3.1 in 1994 and then I saved it on a 💾 diskette

And then my brother overwrote it with Command & Conquer

Then 20 years later I found the diskette, bought a USB diskette reader, used some deep reading diskette recovery app and got my drawing back

Now it's on https://t.co/M1hEUBAynC!

Anyway since then I always had backups of everything, never lost a file
